burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery San Bernardino per FamilySearch

naming_inspiration.description: Third consecutive generation to carry 'Hooker' as a middle name in the Magoffin family — following grandfather James Cole Magoffin's wife Mary Chaffee Hooker and father Rev. James Hooker Magoffin.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; he married on his own birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Frank Hooker Magoffin carried the 'Hooker' middle name his father had established in the family, and married Mary Josephine Scott on June 27, 1881 in Jasper, Iowa — his own twenty-second birthday, a choice that suggests the date was deliberate — before eventually settling in San Bernardino, California, completing the Magoffin family's three-generation migration from New York through Wisconsin through Michigan through Iowa to the Pacific coast. He was the third consecutive Magoffin to bear 'Hooker' as a given name: a thread from his grandmother Mary Chaffee Hooker of Windsor, Connecticut, carried four thousand miles and sixty years to a California desert city.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin (1820-1883), Episcopal priest

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker's maiden name — daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor, CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker to honor his mother's maiden name
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin carried 'Hooker' as his middle name to honor his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker, and passed the tradition forward — his son Frank was named 'Frank Hooker Magoffin,' making three consecutive generations of the Magoffin family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name. His grandson George Magoffin Humphrey became the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower, carrying the heritage in the form of the middle name 'Magoffin.'

narrative_blocks.header: An Episcopal priest in a Presbyterian father's household
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin was ordained as an Episcopal priest despite being raised in a Presbyterian household — his father had been an elder in the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — making him one of the quiet denominational crossovers of mid-nineteenth-century American religion, a son who took his faith in a different direction while preserving his mother's Hooker name in every way he could. He moved west from New York through Wisconsin, where his son Frank Hooker Magoffin was born in Waukesha in 1859, to Cheboygan County, Michigan, dying there in 1883 at sixty-three — following the American frontier even as an Episcopal clergyman.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hill Cemetery Ann Arbor per FA

career.notes: Born in the town of Benton per FA. Served in Michigan area.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hill Cemetery Ann Arbor per FA
First Presbyterian Church Cemetery Schenectady per FA. Inscription: "Elder in the Presbyterian Church who died in his 60th year." Surname listed as "McGaffin" in 1930s rubbings.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived his Hooker wife by eleven years
narrative_blocks.body: Doctor James C. Magoffin was a physician and elder of the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — a man of both medicine and faith in a city becoming one of the first industrial centers of New York State — and outlived his wife Mary Chaffee Hooker Magoffin by eleven years, raising their six children after her death in 1839, three of whom bore her maiden name in their own names. His gravestone records him simply as 'Elder in the Presbyterian Church who died in his 60th year,' the epitaph of a man whose children's naming choices would echo through five generations.

sources.short: Chaffee genealogy book, entry 702 (via mother Mary Chaffee)

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Mary Chaffee' directly for her mother Mary (Chaffee) Hooker (X00319), who was herself named for the Chaffee family.

narrative_blocks.header: Named directly for her mother Mary Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker was named in full for her mother Mary Chaffee Hooker (X00319), making her name a direct echo of the generation before her. She married Dr. James Magoffin of Schenectady and died there on March 20, 1839 at age forty-two, nearly a decade before her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-two; six children survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker Magoffin died on March 27, 1839 in Schenectady at age forty-two, leaving behind Doctor James C. Magoffin and six children, three of whom carried her maiden name 'Hooker' as their middle name — a deliberate act of genealogical memory that would persist through four more generations and eventually surface in the name of the 55th Secretary of the Treasury. She traveled from Windsor, Connecticut to Schenectady and died before seeing any grandchild; but the 'Hooker' she left in her children's names traveled farther than she did.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery Schenectady per FA
